Distributed File Upload System with DevOps Pipeline Lovely Professional University 
1. INTRODUCTION 
In today's digital world, the need to handle large files reliably over a network is a core 
requirement of modern web applications. Traditional file upload approaches that send a file 
in a single HTTP request are fragile — a network interruption causes the entire transfer to 
fail, large files exhaust server memory, and a single server deployment cannot scale to 
handle high traffic. These shortcomings make traditional upload systems unsuitable for 
enterprise use. 
This project, the Distributed File Upload System with DevOps Pipeline, solves these 
problems through two complementary designs: a chunked file transfer mechanism on the 
application side, and a fully automated DevOps pipeline on the operations side. Files of any 
type and size are split into small chunks by the browser, transferred independently, 
reassembled by the Spring Boot backend, and automatically backed up to a private GitHub 
repository — regardless of file size or network conditions. 
On the DevOps side, every code change triggers a GitHub Actions pipeline that 
automatically runs unit tests, packages the application with Maven, builds a Docker image, 
pushes it to Docker Hub, and deploys it to a Kubernetes cluster — achieving continuous 
integration and continuous delivery (CI/CD) with zero manual steps. 
1.1. PROJECT BACKGROUND 
The project was conceived as a practical solution to a real engineering challenge: how to 
reliably upload files larger than 100 MB to a server running in a containerised, replicated 
environment. GitHub's file API limits individual files to 25 MB — which made direct 
storage impossible. The solution was to split merged files into 20 MB parts and push each 
part using the GitHub Contents API, with a manifest file tracking how to reassemble them. 
The DevOps infrastructure was designed in parallel — ensuring that as the application code 
evolved, each new version could be tested, containerised, and deployed automatically 
without developer intervention. 
1.2. PROJECT OBJECTIVES 
•​
Implement chunked file upload in a Spring Boot REST API to handle files of 
arbitrary size. 
•​
Automatically merge received chunks, validate integrity, and push file parts to a 
private GitHub repository. 
•​
Build a CI/CD pipeline: GitHub Actions triggers Maven tests, Docker build/push, 
and Kubernetes deployment on every code commit. 
•​
Deploy the application on Kubernetes (Minikube) with auto-scaling (2–6 pods) and 
rolling updates (zero downtime). 
•​
Build automated validation and reconstruction workflows in the storage repository 
to verify all parts are received correctly and to reconstruct original files as 
downloadable artifacts. 
 
School of Computer Science and Engineering Punit Kumar 
Distributed File Upload System with DevOps PipelineLovely Professional University 
2. SCOPE OF THE STUDY (PROBLEM STATEMENT) 
2.1. PROBLEM STATEMENT 
Traditional file upload systems suffer from several critical limitations that make them 
unsuitable for production environments: 
•​
Single-request failures: One network drop fails the entire upload with no resume 
capability. 
•​
Memory exhaustion: Loading a 500 MB file into server RAM crashes the 
application. 
•​
No redundancy: Files exist only on one server; hardware failure destroys the data 
permanently. 
•​
No integrity checks: Corrupted or incomplete uploads are silently accepted. 
•​
Manual deployment: Developer must SSH into a server and run commands 
manually for every release. 
•​
No auto-scaling: A single server cannot handle sudden spikes in upload traffic. 
 
Additionally, GitHub's Content API enforces a 25 MB per-file limit — making direct 
backup of large files impossible without a split-and-store strategy. 
2.2. SCOPE OF SOLUTION 
This project addresses all of the above by implementing: 
•​
Chunked transfer at the browser layer — only individual chunks need to be re-sent 
on failure. 
•​
Stream-based merging in Spring Boot — no large file is ever loaded fully into 
RAM. 
•​
Automated GitHub backup — every merged file is split into 20 MB parts and 
pushed to a private repo. 
•​
Automated validation workflow — GitHub Actions checks all parts are present 
before reconstruction. 
•​
Fully automated CI/CD — no manual steps between a code commit and a live 
deployment. 
•​
Kubernetes HPA — pods scale automatically from 2 to 6 based on CPU load. 
 
School of Computer Science and Engineering Punit Kumar 
Distributed File Upload System with DevOps PipelineLovely Professional University 
3. SOFTWARE REQUIREMENT ANALYSIS 
5.1. INTRODUCTION 
Software requirements define the functions the system must perform, the constraints it must 
operate under, and the quality attributes it must meet. They are divided into Functional 
Requirements (what the system does) and Non-Functional Requirements (how well it does 
it). 
5.2. GENERAL DESCRIPTION 
The Distributed File Upload System is a web application that allows users to upload files of 
any size and type through a browser. The backend is a Spring Boot application that receives 
file chunks, assembles the original file, validates it, and backs it up to GitHub. The system 
is hosted on a Kubernetes cluster and delivered through a CI/CD pipeline. 
5.3. SPECIFIC REQUIREMENTS 
5.3.1. Functional Requirements 
•​
FR1 — Chunked Upload: The system shall accept files split into numbered chunks 
via HTTP POST to /api/upload. 
•​
FR2 — Chunk Storage: Each chunk shall be stored in a temporary directory indexed 
by fileId and chunkIndex. 
•​
FR3 — Merge: When all expected chunks for a fileId are received, the system shall 
concatenate them in order and produce the original file in uploads/final/. 
•​
FR4 — Validation: The merged file byte count shall be validated against the 
expected total. 
•​
FR5 — GitHub Backup: The merged file shall be split into ≤20 MB parts and each 
part pushed to a private GitHub repository using the Contents API. 
•​
FR6 — Manifest: A text manifest file listing all parts, sizes, and reassembly 
instructions shall be committed alongside the parts. 
•​
FR7 — Validation Workflow: A GitHub Actions workflow shall verify all parts are 
present and sizes are consistent after every push to the storage repository. 
•​
FR8 — Reconstruction: A GitHub Actions workflow shall reconstruct the original 
file from its parts and make it available as a downloadable artifact. 
•​
FR9 — CI/CD: Every push to the main branch of the code repository shall trigger 
an automated pipeline: test → package → docker build → docker push → kubectl 
deploy. 
•​
FR10 — Auto-Scaling: The Kubernetes HPA shall scale pods between 2 and 6 
based on CPU utilisation. 
5.3.2. Non-Functional Requirements 
Category 
Requirement 
Performance 
The system shall handle files up to 1 GB; each 20 MB part shall upload in 
under 30 seconds on a 10 Mbps connection. 
School of Computer Science and EngineeringPunit Kumar 
Distributed File Upload System with DevOps PipelineLovely Professional University 
Category 
Requirement 
Reliability 
File reconstruction shall be byte-perfect (SHA-256 verified). No data loss 
shall occur if a chunk upload fails. 
Scalability 
Kubernetes HPA shall respond to load increases within 60 seconds by adding 
pods. 
Security 
All credentials (PAT, Docker token) shall be stored as GitHub Secrets or 
Kubernetes Secrets — never hardcoded. 
Maintainability 
All infrastructure-as-code (Kubernetes YAML, Dockerfile, workflows) shall 
be version-controlled in Git. 
Portability 
The Docker image shall run identically on Windows, macOS, and Linux. 
Availability 
Rolling update strategy shall ensure zero downtime during deployments 
(maxUnavailable: 0).

Technology 
Version 
Role 
Spring Boot 
3.x 
REST API, chunk management, file merge logic 
Java 
17 LTS 
Primary programming language 
Apache Maven 
3.8+ 
Build tool, dependency management, test runner 
Docker 
24+ 
Application containerisation and image creation 
Docker Hub 
Cloud 
Docker image registry (punitkumar4871/...) 
Kubernetes 
1.28+ 
Container orchestration, scaling, deployment 
Minikube 
1.32+ 
Local Kubernetes cluster for development 
GitHub Actions 
Cloud 
CI/CD pipeline automation 
GitHub Contents API 
v3 
Direct REST API for pushing file parts to GitHub 
Python 
3.10+ 
Validation and merge scripts in storage repo

Distributed File Upload System with DevOps PipelineLovely Professional University 
4. DESIGN 
6.1. SYSTEM DESIGN 
The system is composed of two independent, cooperating pipelines: 
•​
Pipeline A (CI/CD) — Triggered by code commits; handles build, containerisation, 
and deployment. 
•​
Pipeline B (File Storage) — Triggered at runtime by file uploads; handles backup to 
GitHub. 
 
Figure 6.1 — Full System Architecture 
 
  PIPELINE A — CODE DEPLOYMENT (CI/CD) 
  ───────────────────────────────────── 
  Developer pushes code 
        │ 
        ▼ 
  GitHub Actions (Cloud Runner) 
   ├─ mvn test  ──────────────── Unit Tests 
   ├─ mvn package ────────────── JAR file 
   ├─ docker build/push ───────  Docker Hub Image 
        │ 
        ▼ 
  Self-Hosted Runner (Laptop) 
   └─ kubectl apply ──────────── Kubernetes (Minikube) 
                                   ├─ Deployment (2 pods, rolling) 
                                   ├─ Service (LoadBalancer) 
                                   └─ HPA (2–6 pods auto-scale) 
 
  PIPELINE B — FILE STORAGE (Runtime) 
  ────────────────────────────────────── 
  User uploads file (browser chunks) 
        │ 
        ▼ 
  Spring Boot (inside K8s pod) 
   ├─ FileService: save chunks → merge → validate 
   └─ GitService: split 20MB parts → GitHub Contents API 
        │ 
        ▼ 
  Private GitHub Repo (upload-file-storage) 
   ├─ uploads/YYYY/MM/DD/file.part001of3.ext 
   ├─ uploads/YYYY/MM/DD/file.part002of3.ext 
   ├─ uploads/YYYY/MM/DD/file.part003of3.ext 
   └─ uploads/YYYY/MM/DD/file.ext.manifest.txt 
        │ 
        ▼ 
  auto-merge.yml triggers 
   ├─ scan_uploads.py  → detect complete sets 
   ├─ validate.py      → verify sizes + integrity 
   └─ merge.py         → reconstruct + SHA-256 + artifact 
6.2. DESIGN NOTATIONS 
6.2.1. Module Breakdown 
School of Computer Science and EngineeringPunit Kumar 
Distributed File Upload System with DevOps PipelineLovely Professional University 
Module 
File 
Responsibility 
Upload Controller 
ChunkUploadController.java 
REST endpoint; receives chunks; calls 
FileService 
File Service 
FileService.java 
Saves chunks; detects completion; merges; 
validates 
Git Service 
GitService.java 
Splits merged file; pushes parts via 
GitHub API 
Scan Script 
scan_uploads.py 
Finds complete upload sets; skips 
already-merged 
Validate Script 
validate.py 
Checks all parts present; verifies sizes 
Merge Script 
merge.py 
Reconstructs file; computes SHA-256; 
creates artifact 
CI/CD Workflow 
ci-cd.yml 
Build → Test → Docker → K8s deploy on 
code push 
Auto-Merge Workflow 
auto-merge.yml 
Validate + merge on push to uploads/**

         ┌───────────────────────┐

                    ▼                           │

         │ All chunks received?  │ ────────────┘

         ┌───────────────────────┐ 
         │  Merge chunks in order │

         │ Commit manifest file   │ 
         └──────────┬────────────┘

6.4.2. CI/CD Pipeline Flowchart 
 
Figure 6.5 — CI/CD Pipeline Flowchart 
  Developer  ──▶  git push main 
                       │ 
                       ▼ 
              GitHub Actions triggers 
                       │ 
            ┌──────────┴───────────┐ 
            │  JOB 1: Build & Test  │ 
            │  mvn test             │ 
            │  mvn package          │ 
            └──────────┬────────────┘ 
                       │ 
              Tests pass? 
            YES │        │ NO 
                ▼        ▼ 
            Continue  Pipeline FAILS 
                │     (notification sent) 
                ▼ 
     ┌──────────────────────────┐ 
     │  JOB 2: Docker Build     │ 
     │  docker build -t image   │ 
     │  docker push to Hub      │ 
     └────────────┬─────────────┘ 
    ▼ 
     ┌──────────────────────────┐ 
     │  JOB 3: K8s Deploy       │ 
     │  (self-hosted runner)    │ 
     │  kubectl apply manifests │ 
     │  kubectl rollout status  │ 
     └────────────┬─────────────┘ 
    ▼ 
App live on Minikube

6. SYSTEM SNAPSHOTS AND SOURCE CODE 
The following snapshots describe the key screens and log outputs observed during the 
project. Actual screenshots should be inserted at the indicated positions. 
10.1. FILE UPLOAD UI 
 
The browser-side upload interface shows a file picker and a progress bar. JavaScript splits 
the selected file into chunks and sends each independently via POST requests. 
10.2. SPRING BOOT TERMINAL LOGS 
Figure 10.1 — Spring Boot Terminal Log During EXE Upload 
[FileService] Saved chunk 229 for fileId=662x6fqx4mpf2kgr0 
[FileService] Saved chunk 230 for fileId=662x6fqx4mpf2kgr0 
[FileService] Saved chunk 231 for fileId=662x6fqx4mpf2kgr0 
[FileService] Merged 232 chunks → uploads\final\MSB (1).exe 
[FileService] Deleted temp dir for fileId=662x6fqx4mpf2kgr0 
School of Computer Science and EngineeringPunit Kumar 
Distributed File Upload System with DevOps PipelineLovely Professional University 
[GitService] ====== GITHUB PUSH ====== 
[GitService] File        : MSB (1).exe 
[GitService] Token set   : YES (ghp_eTtb...) 
[GitService] Repo        : punitkumar4871/upload-file-storage 
[GitService] File size   : 231.6 MB 
[GitService] Splitting into 12 x 20MB parts... 
[GitService] Part  1/12 (20.0 MB) → committed OK 
[GitService] Part  2/12 (20.0 MB) → committed OK 
  ...  (parts 3–11 committed OK) 
[GitService] Part 12/12 (11.6 MB) → committed OK 
[GitService] Manifest committed: uploads/2026/05/21/MSB_1.exe.manifest.txt 
[GitService] Result: 12/12 parts committed. 
10.3. GITHUB ACTIONS — AUTO-MERGE WORKFLOW 
Figure 10.2 — GitHub Actions Auto-Merge Workflow Output 
  ✅  JOB 1: DETECT 
      [SCAN] MSB (1).exe  — ALREADY MERGED — skipping 
      [SCAN] 50mb.pdf     — Parts found: 3/3 — COMPLETE 
      Files ready to merge: 1 
 
  ✅  JOB 2: VALIDATE 
      Part  1/3: 50mb.part001of3.pdf  (20.0 MB) 
      Part  2/3: 50mb.part002of3.pdf  (20.0 MB) 
      Part  3/3: 50mb.part003of3.pdf  (12.1 MB) 
      RESULT: PASSED — all checks OK 
 
  ✅  JOB 3: MERGE 
      Merging 3 parts → 50mb.pdf 
      SHA-256: a3f8e2...9b1c 
      Artifact uploaded: merged-files-47.zip 
      Checksum committed: merged/2026/05/21/50mb.pdf.sha256
